title: Te Kōrero o te Awa: The Living Voice of Mangakōtukutuku Stream / Te Reo o te Taiao: The Language of Our Environment
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore the traditional stories and environmental significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the stream where the bellbird drinks), which flows through our rohe. They will learn how Ngāti Kahungunu ancestors used specific reo to describe the stream's health, resources, and spiritual significance, connecting language learning to our local waterway's past, present, and future.
learningObjectives: Identify and use at least 10 Māori kupu related to water ecosystems (awa, tuna, inanga, repo, etc.) in spoken and written contexts
Recount the pūrākau (traditional story) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using appropriate sentence structures and sequencing language
Create a simple pepeha (introduction) that includes personal connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Ask and answer basic questions about the health and features of our local awa using te reo Māori

activities.title: He Whakamīharo te Awa - Discovering Our Amazing Stream
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua Matua Hone Smith. They learn the pūrākau of the awa while identifying and naming key features (wai, rocks, plants, birds) using te reo Māori. Students create simple audio recordings of water sounds and Māori names for each feature.

activities.title: Ngā Kupu o te Taiao - Language of Our Environment
activities.description: Using waterproof cards and digital apps, students participate in a 'kupu hunt' around the awa, matching Māori vocabulary with physical features. They work in pairs to create simple sentences describing what they see (e.g., 'Kei te rere te wai,' 'He nui te tuna') and practice these with peers.

activities.title: Taku Pepeha, Taku Awa - My Introduction, My Stream
activities.description: Students develop a personal pepeha that includes their connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. They learn traditional structures and incorporate specific references to the stream, practicing with templates and peer support before recording their final pepeha.

activities.title: He Pātai, He Whakautu - Questions and Answers About Our Awa
activities.description: Using question frameworks learned in class, students interview each other about their observations and feelings toward the awa. They practice asking and answering questions about the stream's health, beauty, and importance using basic sentence structures and newly acquired vocabulary.

activities.title: Te Whakaora o te Awa - Our Stream's Restoration
activities.description: Students create simple bilingual signs (Māori/English) for placement near the awa, using appropriate vocabulary and phrases to encourage protection of the stream. They present their signs to the class and explain their word choices in te reo Māori.

resources: Matua Hone Smith (Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and native speaker)
Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku field trip access and safety equipment
Māori vocabulary cards and waterproof learning materials from Te Taura Whiri i te Reo Māori
Digital recording devices and te reo learning apps (e.g., Kupu by Google)

tasks: Recorded pepeha including personal connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Completed vocabulary matching exercise with 10+ environmental kupu
Simple dialogue demonstration asking/answering questions about the awa
Bilingual conservation sign creation and explanation

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 3: Use basic Māori language patterns and vocabulary to communicate about immediate environment
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Level 3: Recognize and understand some common Māori vocabulary and sentence structures
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways
Health and Physical Education: Community resources for well-being
